subtopic_detail: Gestational diabetes screening 75 g oral glucose tolerance test thresholds
stem: You are the antenatal clinic registrar reviewing a 34-year-old woman at 27+5 weeks gestation in her second pregnancy. Her first pregnancy was uncomplicated and her booking body mass index was 26 kg/m^2. She has no family history of diabetes. She fasted overnight and underwent her routine 75 g oral glucose tolerance test this morning. The fetus is appropriately grown by symphysiofundal height and ultrasound at 20 weeks was normal.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender uterus, fundal height 27 cm, fetal movements present
data_table.Investigations: 75 g OGTT: fasting plasma glucose 5.4 mmol/L, 1-hour 9.6 mmol/L, 2-hour 7.8 mmol/L. HbA1c 5.5%. Urinalysis negative.

lead_in: How should this result be interpreted and managed?

options.text: Results are normal; reassure her and book routine antenatal follow-up at 32 weeks with serial growth scans not currently indicated by the present screening result
options.rationale: Her 1-hour value of 9.6 mmol/L is above the ADIPS threshold of 8.5 mmol/L and meets criteria for gestational diabetes; describing the test as normal is incorrect. (Source: ADIPS Gestational Diabetes Consensus)

options.text: Diagnose gestational diabetes, refer to the antenatal endocrinology team, commence home glucose monitoring and provide dietitian input and exercise advice this week
options.rationale: A single value at or above the ADIPS thresholds (fasting 5.1, 1-hour 10.0, 2-hour 8.5 mmol/L) confirms gestational diabetes; multidisciplinary care with self-monitoring and lifestyle measures is standard first-line management. (Source: ADIPS Gestational Diabetes Consensus)

options.text: Repeat the 75 g oral glucose tolerance test at 32 weeks because intermediate values do not meet thresholds and confirmatory testing is needed before any diagnosis can be made
options.rationale: Australian criteria do not require repeat testing when any one value is above threshold; deferring action loses critical time for glycaemic optimisation. (Source: ADIPS Gestational Diabetes Consensus)

options.text: Start metformin 500 mg twice daily immediately as universal first-line pharmacotherapy and bypass dietary and lifestyle counselling at this stage of pregnancy and care
options.rationale: First-line management of gestational diabetes is lifestyle modification with home glucose monitoring; pharmacotherapy is added if targets are not met after 1 to 2 weeks of intervention. (Source: ADIPS Gestational Diabetes Consensus)

options.text: Order a subsequent HbA1c at 28 weeks and treat only if it rises above 5.7%, as glucose tolerance testing is increasingly considered less specific in pregnancy assessment
options.rationale: HbA1c is not used to make a gestational diabetes diagnosis after 20 weeks in Australian practice; the 75 g oral glucose tolerance test remains the standard. (Source: ADIPS Gestational Diabetes Consensus)

explanation.summary: Gestational diabetes is diagnosed by a 75 g oral glucose tolerance test performed between 24 and 28 weeks gestation, using the ADIPS thresholds of fasting 5.1, 1-hour 10.0 and 2-hour 8.5 mmol/L. A single value above threshold establishes the diagnosis. First-line management is lifestyle modification with home capillary glucose monitoring before adding insulin or metformin. Untreated gestational diabetes increases macrosomia, shoulder dystocia, preterm birth and operative delivery rates.
key_points: ADIPS thresholds: fasting 5.1, 1-hour 10.0, 2-hour 8.5 mmol/L; one value above threshold is diagnostic
Lifestyle modification is first line for at least 1 to 2 weeks before adding pharmacotherapy
Insulin remains first-line pharmacotherapy in Australia though metformin is increasingly accepted
Targets are fasting under 5.0 mmol/L and 1-hour postprandial under 7.4 mmol/L
Postpartum 75 g oral glucose tolerance test is repeated at 6 to 12 weeks to detect persisting diabetes
explanation.pearls: Women diagnosed with gestational diabetes have a 50% lifetime risk of progressing to type 2 diabetes and should have annual fasting glucose or 2-yearly oral glucose tolerance testing postpartum.

subtopic_detail: Placental abruption with maternal compromise and concealed haemorrhage
stem: You are the obstetric registrar called urgently to triage a 31-year-old multiparous woman at 34+2 weeks with sudden severe abdominal pain. She had a small painful vaginal bleed 30 minutes ago and is now pale and clammy. She has had no leaking fluid. Her booking blood pressure has been 110 to 120 systolic; she has no history of trauma. She is rhesus D positive.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 °C, HR 122, BP 86/52, RR 24, SpO2 99% room air, capillary refill 4 seconds
data_table.Examination: Tense, tender uterus, fundal height 36 cm, persistent firm contraction without relaxation, no urinary catheter output yet
data_table.Investigations: CTG: fetal heart rate baseline 90 bpm with late decelerations and reduced variability. FBC pending. Bedside ultrasound: posterior placenta with no previa.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence intravenous magnesium sulfate 4 g loading dose for fetal neuroprotection and continue an ongoing maintenance infusion before any other intervention is initiated
options.rationale: Magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection is appropriate when delivery is anticipated under 30 weeks; at 34 weeks it is no longer indicated, and resuscitation and delivery must not be delayed. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Insert two large-bore intravenous cannulae, take group and screen with a clotting profile and admit for 24 hours of continuous fetal heart rate monitoring observation only
options.rationale: Maternal shock with a non-reassuring cardiotocograph at this gestation mandates urgent delivery rather than expectant monitoring; ongoing concealed haemorrhage will worsen. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Administer betamethasone 11.4 mg intramuscular and tocolyse with nifedipine 20 mg orally to prolong the pregnancy to 36 weeks while monitoring blood loss carefully
options.rationale: Tocolysis is contraindicated in placental abruption with maternal or fetal compromise; prolonging pregnancy risks progressive concealed haemorrhage, coagulopathy and intrauterine death. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Activate the massive transfusion protocol, commence intravenous resuscitation with warmed crystalloid and group O Rh-negative blood and proceed to category 1 caesarean section
options.rationale: The picture of haemodynamic compromise, tetanic uterus and fetal bradycardia indicates major placental abruption; immediate resuscitation with category 1 caesarean section is required to deliver the fetus and address ongoing concealed haemorrhage. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Order an urgent magnetic resonance imaging of the abdomen and pelvis to formally quantify the retroplacental haematoma before deciding on the mode and timing of delivery
options.rationale: Placental abruption is a clinical diagnosis; advanced imaging delays delivery and does not change management in the setting of shock and fetal compromise. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage)

explanation.summary: Placental abruption is a clinical diagnosis suggested by painful vaginal bleeding, tense tender uterus and fetal compromise. Concealed haemorrhage means external blood loss underestimates total loss; maternal observations and fetal status are more reliable. Resuscitation, massive transfusion activation and category 1 caesarean section are the time-critical steps. Disseminated intravascular coagulation complicates around 10% of significant abruptions and is anticipated with empirical fibrinogen and platelet replacement.
key_points: Concealed haemorrhage means external blood loss underestimates true blood loss
Tetanic uterus and fetal bradycardia indicate major abruption requiring urgent delivery
Tocolysis is contraindicated when maternal or fetal compromise is present
Massive transfusion activation should be early, not after coagulopathy is confirmed
Disseminated intravascular coagulation complicates around 10% of significant abruptions
explanation.pearls: Fibrinogen below 2 g/L is the earliest and most sensitive coagulation marker for abruption-related disseminated intravascular coagulation and should trigger cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate replacement.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar conducting a vaginal delivery for a 32-year-old multiparous woman at 39+4 weeks. The pregnancy has been complicated by diet-controlled gestational diabetes and the estimated fetal weight at 38 weeks was 4.1 kg. After delivery of the head the chin retracts firmly against the perineum and the anterior shoulder does not deliver with routine downward traction. The time of head delivery has been noted aloud.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal: T 36.7 °C, HR 102, BP 120/78. Fetal heart rate immediately pre-event reassuring on CTG.
data_table.Examination: Turtle sign with chin retraction, head restitution incomplete, no perineal lacerations

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Call for help, flatten the bed, hyperflex the maternal hips onto the abdomen and apply firm suprapubic pressure from the side of the fetal back
options.rationale: McRoberts manoeuvre with suprapubic pressure is the first-line intervention in the HELPERR sequence and resolves around 40 to 60% of shoulder dystocia cases without further escalation. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Apply continued strong downward axial traction on the fetal head while an assistant applies fundal pressure from above to push the anterior shoulder out
options.rationale: Fundal pressure worsens shoulder impaction and increases the risk of uterine rupture and brachial plexus injury; downward traction beyond gentle routine traction risks brachial plexus injury. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Proceed immediately to symphysiotomy under local anaesthetic to widen the maternal pelvic outlet and allow delivery of the impacted anterior shoulder rapidly
options.rationale: Symphysiotomy is a last-resort intervention reserved for failed internal manoeuvres and Zavanelli; it is not the first-line approach. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Replace the fetal head into the pelvis with Zavanelli manoeuvre and arrange immediate transfer to theatre for delivery by category 1 caesarean section as the next step
options.rationale: The Zavanelli manoeuvre is reserved for failed internal rotational manoeuvres and posterior arm delivery; it is not the first step in shoulder dystocia management. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Administer intravenous oxytocin 5 international units bolus and oral nifedipine 20 mg to relax the uterus and facilitate the anterior shoulder coming under the symphysis
options.rationale: Uterotonics and tocolytics have no role in the first-line manoeuvres for shoulder dystocia; the problem is mechanical impaction, not uterine activity. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

explanation.summary: Shoulder dystocia is an unpredictable obstetric emergency where downward traction fails to deliver the anterior shoulder after the head. The HELPERR mnemonic structures the response: call for Help, Evaluate for episiotomy, Legs in McRoberts, suprapubic Pressure, Enter manoeuvres, Remove posterior arm and Roll the patient. McRoberts plus suprapubic pressure resolves around 40 to 60% of cases. Fundal pressure is contraindicated and downward axial traction must remain gentle to avoid brachial plexus injury.
key_points: McRoberts and suprapubic pressure are the first-line manoeuvres in HELPERR
Fundal pressure is contraindicated and risks uterine rupture and brachial plexus injury
Time the head delivery and call for senior help immediately
Internal manoeuvres such as Rubin or Woods screw follow if McRoberts fails
Document time intervals, manoeuvres performed and personnel present
explanation.pearls: Brachial plexus injuries occur in 4 to 16% of shoulder dystocia cases but most resolve fully by 12 months; counsel parents about the favourable prognosis to reduce psychological harm.

subtopic_detail: Postmenopausal bleeding investigation and endometrial sampling
stem: You are the gynaecology registrar reviewing a 62-year-old woman who reports two episodes of light vaginal bleeding over six weeks. Her last menstrual period was at age 50 and she has had no hormonal therapy. Her body mass index is 34 kg/m^2 and she has type 2 diabetes. She has had no abdominal pain or weight loss. She has no family history of gynaecological malignancy. Bimanual examination is unremarkable.

data_table.Examination: Speculum: small amount of old blood in vagina, cervix appears healthy. Bimanual: anteverted mobile non-tender uterus.
data_table.Investigations: Transvaginal ultrasound: endometrial thickness 9 mm, heterogeneous endometrium, no obvious focal lesion. Cervical screening up to date and negative. FBC and ferritin unremarkable.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat the transvaginal ultrasound in 8 weeks and consider further investigation only if the bleeding persists or the endometrial thickness measurement increases above 12 mm
options.rationale: Postmenopausal bleeding requires histological sampling because endometrial cancer can occur with thicknesses above 4 mm and watchful waiting risks delayed diagnosis. (Source: RANZCOG: Postmenopausal Bleeding)

options.text: Start systemic oral progestogen therapy with norethisterone 5 mg three times daily for three months and reassess the bleeding pattern at the end of that course
options.rationale: Empirical progestogen therapy does not address the diagnostic question and can mask underlying endometrial malignancy; sampling is required first. (Source: RANZCOG: Postmenopausal Bleeding)

options.text: Refer for outpatient hysteroscopy with directed endometrial sampling because the endometrial thickness is above 4 mm and risk factors for endometrial carcinoma are present
options.rationale: Any postmenopausal bleeding with endometrial thickness above 4 mm on transvaginal ultrasound requires hysteroscopic visualisation and directed sampling; obesity and diabetes raise the pretest probability further. (Source: RANZCOG: Postmenopausal Bleeding)

options.text: Order pelvic magnetic resonance imaging now to evaluate for myometrial invasion before any histological sampling is performed and refer to gynaecological oncology
options.rationale: Magnetic resonance imaging is used for staging once endometrial cancer is histologically confirmed, not as a primary diagnostic test. (Source: RANZCOG: Postmenopausal Bleeding)

options.text: Reassure her that an endometrial thickness under 10 mm in a postmenopausal woman is reassuring and arrange a routine general practitioner review in 6 to 8 weeks
options.rationale: An endometrial thickness above 4 mm in a woman with postmenopausal bleeding mandates sampling regardless of the patient demographics; reassurance is unsafe. (Source: RANZCOG: Postmenopausal Bleeding)

explanation.summary: Postmenopausal bleeding is endometrial cancer until proven otherwise. The transvaginal ultrasound endometrial thickness cutoff is 4 mm in Australian practice; women above this threshold require hysteroscopic visualisation with directed endometrial sampling. Risk factors include obesity, type 2 diabetes, unopposed oestrogen exposure, tamoxifen use and Lynch syndrome. Endometrioid adenocarcinoma carries a favourable prognosis when caught at FIGO stage 1, with overall 5-year survival around 90%.
key_points: Postmenopausal bleeding is endometrial cancer until proven otherwise
Endometrial thickness above 4 mm on transvaginal ultrasound mandates histological sampling
Obesity, diabetes, unopposed oestrogen, tamoxifen and Lynch syndrome raise risk
Hysteroscopy with directed biopsy is preferred over blind pipelle when ultrasound is abnormal
Recurrent bleeding after a normal pipelle still requires hysteroscopic re-evaluation
explanation.pearls: Lynch syndrome (hereditary non-polyposis colorectal cancer) accounts for around 2 to 5% of endometrial cancers and should be considered when diagnosis occurs under age 50 or with a family history of colorectal cancer.

subtopic_detail: Primary postpartum haemorrhage with continued atonic bleeding after oxytocin
stem: You are the obstetric registrar called to a 28-year-old primigravida 20 minutes after a normal vaginal delivery at 40+1 weeks. Estimated blood loss is now 1300 mL and continues briskly. The placenta has been delivered intact, the perineum has no significant lacerations and the bladder is empty. She received intramuscular oxytocin 10 international units at delivery and the uterus remains poorly contracted on examination. Her booking blood pressure was 110/70 with no history of hypertension.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 °C, HR 118, BP 92/58, RR 22, SpO2 98% room air
data_table.Examination: Soft boggy uterus above the umbilicus, bladder empty, vagina with steady fresh blood loss, no cervical or vaginal lacerations
data_table.Investigations: FBC and coagulation studies have been sent; group and screen is current

lead_in: After uterine massage, what is the most appropriate next uterotonic step?

options.text: Administer rectal misoprostol 800 micrograms with continuation of crystalloid resuscitation while awaiting return of intravenous access for ongoing fluid therapy
options.rationale: Misoprostol is slower onset than parenteral uterotonics and is reserved for settings with no other agents available; intravenous oxytocin and ergometrine are first-line. (Source: RANZCOG: Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Activate the massive transfusion protocol and transfer to theatre immediately for examination under anaesthesia, balloon tamponade and possible B-Lynch suture insertion
options.rationale: Surgical and tamponade interventions follow when pharmacological uterotonics fail; the next step after oxytocin is ergometrine and tranexamic acid before escalation. (Source: RANZCOG: Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Start a syntocinon infusion at 40 international units in 1000 mL of normal saline and avoid ergometrine because of her body mass index and current blood pressure measurement
options.rationale: Body mass index is not a contraindication to ergometrine; her blood pressure is low rather than high, so ergometrine is appropriate. (Source: RANZCOG: Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Administer intramuscular carboprost 250 micrograms repeated every 15 minutes up to a maximum of 8 doses and proceed to theatre if uterine atony persists despite the doses
options.rationale: Carboprost is a third-line uterotonic used after ergometrine has failed or is contraindicated; jumping to it skips a step in the Australian algorithm. (Source: RANZCOG: Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Give ergometrine 250 micrograms intramuscularly and intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes alongside an oxytocin infusion and prepare for theatre if bleeding continues
options.rationale: After initial oxytocin fails, the Australian PPH ladder is ergometrine with tranexamic acid 1 g (given within 3 hours of birth) plus an oxytocin infusion; prepare for theatre if bleeding continues. (Source: RANZCOG: Postpartum Haemorrhage)

explanation.summary: Primary postpartum haemorrhage above 1000 mL or with haemodynamic compromise requires a stepwise pharmacological and surgical response. The Australian ladder is uterine massage and oxytocin, then ergometrine, tranexamic acid and an oxytocin infusion, then carboprost or misoprostol, and finally tamponade and surgical haemostasis. Tranexamic acid within 3 hours of delivery reduces death from postpartum haemorrhage by around 30%. Ergometrine is avoided only with pre-eclampsia or pre-existing hypertension.
key_points: Uterine atony is the cause of around 70% of primary postpartum haemorrhage cases
Ergometrine 250 micrograms is the second-line uterotonic unless hypertension is present
Tranexamic acid 1 g intravenous within 3 hours reduces postpartum haemorrhage mortality by around 30%
Carboprost is contraindicated in asthma because of bronchospasm risk
Bakri balloon tamponade controls bleeding before laparotomy and uterine compression sutures
explanation.pearls: Fibrinogen falls early in postpartum haemorrhage; a level below 2 g/L predicts severe haemorrhage and warrants early cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate replacement.
